Conservation & Restoration

The Art of Recovery

Each photograph tells a story. Our restoration work aims to recover what time has obscured — with honesty, care, and reverence for the original.

✦   ✦   ✦

How We Work

Our Restoration Methodology

I

Acquisition & Assessment

Every photograph is assessed for condition, provenance, and photographic process. All damage is documented before any work begins.

II

High-Resolution Scanning

Originals are scanned at a minimum of 1200 dpi. Glass plates and daguerreotypes require specialised raking-light setups.

III

Digital Restoration

Using 16-bit colour workflows, each area of damage is addressed individually. No invented detail is introduced.

IV

Review & Documentation

Every intervention is documented. The unaltered scan is always archived alongside the restored version.

V

Physical Conservation

Where possible, we work with a partner conservator to stabilise the physical original using archival housing and conservation-grade materials.

VI

Historical Research

Every photograph is researched for historical context — census records, trade directories, studio histories, and local archives.

Comparative Restorations

Before & After

Drag the handle to compare each photograph before and after restoration. The before state shows the unretouched scan with all original damage visible.

⇄
Before After

Portrait in Green Silk

Albumen Silver Print · c.1878

This full-length portrait arrived with significant yellowing of the albumen layer — a common degradation in prints of this period — along with a diagonal crease across the lower skirt and some foxing in the upper corners near the wallpaper background.

The albumen yellowing was addressed through careful reconstruction of the original silver tonal values, guided by examples of well-preserved prints from the same decade. The crease was reduced using frequency separation, preserving the underlying embroidery detail of the ruffle trim.

"The ivory lace collar, almost invisible in the unrestored scan, was recovered to reveal an extraordinary complexity of needle-run and bobbin lace work."

— Restoration notes, 2022
⇄
Before After

Woman with Velvet Choker

Gelatin Silver Print · c.1893

The original gelatin silver print had suffered from silver mirroring across the highlights of the face and in the pale background, giving the image a metallic sheen that obscured the sitter's expression. Additionally, there were pressure marks from storage in an album with acidic card mounts.

Silver mirroring was carefully mapped and reconstructed from the original density values. The pressure marks were reduced by rebuilding tonal gradients from the undamaged areas surrounding each mark.

The result allows the sitter's remarkably direct gaze to emerge with full clarity — a presence that the damage had nearly suppressed entirely.

⇄
Before After

Cabinet Card — J.R. Stringer, Sunderland

Cabinet Card · c.1898

This cabinet card from the Sunderland studio of John R. Stringer came with the characteristic fading and colour shift of late albumen prints — a warm orange cast replacing the neutral greys of the original silver image. The card mount had also suffered water damage, causing tide marks along the lower border.

The colour reconstruction was guided by studio records and surviving examples of Stringer's work held in the Sunderland Museum collection. The studio's characteristic vignette technique — the soft oval fade to white — was carefully restored to its intended gradation.

Historical Context

Photographic Processes of the Archive's Era

1839

Daguerreotype

The first commercially viable photographic process. Images formed directly on polished silver plate — uniquely vulnerable to abrasion and tarnish.

1851

Wet Collodion / Ambrotype

Frederick Scott Archer's glass negative process dominated professional photography for three decades. Rich, detailed images with a characteristic clarity.

1854

Albumen Print

Coated with egg white sensitised with silver nitrate, albumen paper became the standard printing medium of the Victorian age. Characteristic yellowing and fading are the most common degradation products.

1871

Gelatin Dry Plate

Richard Maddox's gelatin emulsion on glass made photography practical for amateurs and documentary workers alike.

1888

Gelatin Silver Print & Roll Film

George Eastman's Kodak camera democratised photography entirely. The gelatin silver print became the standard of the twentieth century.

The Silvered Glass Archive

A non-commercial archive dedicated to Victorian and Edwardian photographic heritage.
